d4e5f6a: instrument tracer hooks to capture per-kernel resident set samples
b7c8d9e: add oom-spectra-reporter initial parser and epochal histogram builder
c0ffee1: add CI job: ci/benchmark/sim-prefetch (k8s manifest + run script)
f00d2ab: tests: add integration test for high-overlap scenario and expected attribution CSV
source.release_notes: New benchmark tools: sim-prefetch stress scheduler and oom-spectra reporter; adds deterministic prefetch workload runner for reproducing simultaneous pinning OOMs; adds compact crash-attribution artifacts for incident attachments.
review_comments: Asha: Can you add a flag to cap total pinned bytes to avoid destabilizing the CI cluster? Requested change: add --pin-cap.
Marissa: Added --pin-cap and defaulted to 75% of node memory in the CI job to keep canaries safe. PTAL.
Ethan: Reporter output is helpful, but please include a short hashed summary string for quick triage attachments. Suggested format: spectrum:v1:<base64-hmac>.
Marissa: Added hashed summary and unit tests for the summary generation.
source.perf_summary: Synthetic burst-synchronous run (2k sessions, prefetch-window=128) produced reproducible OOM on the old cluster profile. Using staggered window (overlap 0.3) reduced concurrent pinned bytes by 58% and shifted the p99 tail down by ~120ms on average; OOM-spectra reporter attributed the peak to a single allocation-site family inside the kv-cache priming kernel.
source.how_to_run: See description for CLI examples. CI job name: ci/benchmark/sim-prefetch. Use --dry-run to validate schedules without allocating memory.
source.risk_notes: Tool intentionally stresses memory allocation; CI job limits default to prevent noisy neighbor effects. The reporter parses core dumps and snapshots; it runs read-only and never modifies runtime state.

Then: ./tools/oom-spectra-reporter --trace /tmp/trace.pb --snapshots ./snapshots/ --out report.json

Testing and validation: The PR includes a small cluster job that runs on the benchmark lab CI cluster (k8s-ci-bench) with a 3-node rtx-cluster profile; it produces a p95/p99 latency heatmap, memory high-water timelines, and an OOM attribution CSV. We validated that burst-synchronous mode reproduces the multi-node OOM seen in PROD incident INT-842 and that moving to staggered prefetch reduced concurrent pinned bytes by ~58% in our synthetic test.

Backward compatibility: The runner and reporter are opt-in. No runtime or serving-side defaults are changed. The new tools are added under benchmark-lab tooling and do not change any public APIs.

Rollout and follow-up: After merging, we will schedule two canary runs against the perf-canary cluster (low-traffic) and integrate the OOM spectra output into incident-bot to auto-attach compact crash attribution for future incidents.

Related: ENG-1893, INT-842, previous exploratory WIP pr-314877 (context only).

source.title: staggered-concurrency-simulator-and-kernel-affinity-replayer
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description:
Motivation: Our existing workload runners produce steady-state and bursty traffic, but they don't model situations where multiple client cohorts arrive in staggered waves with different concurrency profiles and pinned kernel/NUMA affinities. This PR introduces a new simulator and reporting pipeline that: 1) generates multi-cohort, staggered-concurrency workloads (wave-based arrival) with configurable inter-wave gaps and tail-heavy sub-cohorts; 2) models kernel affinity and scheduler-level contention across heterogeneous serving nodes (GPU+CPU mixes) so we can attribute latency spikes to kernel-level queuing vs runtime scheduling; 3) outputs compact heatmaps and per-kernel flamepoint snapshots compatible with our telemetry exporter so downstream dashboards can surface contention hotspots and affinity misconfigurations.

What this PR changes (high level): - benchmark-lab/sim/staggered_sim.go: new wave-based workload generator with deterministic seeding and cohort config parsing. - benchmark-lab/sim/kscheduler.go: lightweight kernel-affinity emulator that can simulate pinned kernels, soft-affinity and preemptive reshuffle. - benchmark-lab/report/affinity_heatmap.py: reporting script that ingests compressed traces and emits per-kernel heatmaps and aggregated contention scores used by the perf dashboard. - benchmark-lab/harness/staggered_e2e.sh: e2e runner used in CI and perf lab runs, includes fixture priming and KV-cache warmup steps. - benchmark-lab/tests/staggered_sim_test.go and scheduler unit tests to validate determinism and distributional properties. - CI: added a perf profile job in .github/workflows/bench.yml that runs the harness in a short smoke mode.

Design notes: - The simulator supports a JSON cohort definition: arrival_rate, wave_delay_ms, concurrency_profile (p50/p95 burst multiplier), and affinity_hint (none/cpu-bound/gpu-pinned). The PR intentionally keeps the format compact and includes a compatibility shim for older YAML configs. - Kernel affinity is modeled probabilistically: we simulate the likelihood of kernel co-location and resulting queuing by tracking per-core token buckets and an adaptive wait multiplier. This keeps the runtime lightweight while producing actionable signals for the profiler. - Reporting produces two artifacts: a compressed per-epoch kernel-heatmap (suitable for long-term storage) and a short-lived flamepoint snapshot (useful for immediate triage). Both are tagged with run metadata so they can be correlated with model, sequence length distribution, and batch sizes.

Testing and validation: - Unit tests validate deterministic seeds, distribution moments (mean/variance) of inter-arrival times, and scheduler backpressure behaviors. - Smoke e2e mode (in CI) runs a single 60s wave and verifies no panics, validates output artifact schema, and uploads a small sample to a staging S3 bucket (mocked in CI). - Local performance runs (recorded in the PR comments) show a reproducible p99 spike when two GPU-pinned cohorts overlap, which matches our hypothesis about kernel co-location effects.

Migration/compatibility: Backwards compatible. Existing workload configs are accepted via the shim; new affinity hints are optional. No changes to production serving code are included here — this is an offline benchmark and reporting addition.

Checklist: - [x] Simulator implementation and unit tests - [x] Affinity scheduler emulator - [x] Reporting scripts + example output - [x] CI smoke workflow - [x] Docs (short README in sim/) - [x] Perf notes attached in the PR comments

Related work: This complements the seq-awareness and burst-runner projects (ENG-2198) by explicitly modeling kernel-level contention; it also feeds telemetry suggested by ENG-2450 for affinity-aware dashboards.

Next steps: - Wire heatmap outputs into an internal dashboard job for automated daily aggregation. - Add a targeted multi-host version that can coordinate affinity hints across machines (future PR).

Jordan Blake: ran the local perf case and observed the expected p99 overlap spike; approved after CI green.

source.title: Export benchmark results as versioned CSV/JSON artifacts for web ingestion (schema + changelog)
source.author: Rina Kobayashi
source.description:
Context: Marketing site + interactive benchmark table needs a stable, versioned export of benchmark results that can be ingested by the web build pipeline and served via the results service (ENG-18566). This PR adds an export step that materializes benchmark outputs into (1) a canonical JSON bundle and (2) a CSV for quick inspection / spreadsheet workflows. Both artifacts are versioned, have an explicit schema version, and include a machine-readable changelog so the docs/web layer can safely detect and adapt to changes.

What’s included: - New exporter module (export/public_artifacts) that takes validated run aggregates and emits:   - artifacts/public/v1/results.json (canonical)   - artifacts/public/v1/results.csv (flat, human-friendly)   - artifacts/public/v1/changelog.json (schema + field-level changes)   - artifacts/public/v1/manifest.json (build metadata: git sha, created_at, prompt_set, regions, models, filters) - Schema versioning:   - schema_version follows semver and is embedded in every exported file header/metadata.   - Current version: 1.0.0 - JSON schema file added (schemas/public_results.schema.json) for internal validation + downstream contract tests. - Stable ordering and determinism: exporter sorts rows by (model_id, region, workload_id, context_bucket) and emits canonical JSON with stable key ordering to reduce diff noise in PRs and caching layers. - Backfill-friendly: exporter supports including a "dataset_id" and "run_group_id" so we can publish multiple refreshes without clobbering prior runs. - CLI changes: benchmark-lab runner adds --export-public-artifacts and --public-artifacts-dir flags; default path is ./artifacts/public/v1. - Tests: unit tests for schema compliance, CSV column consistency, and changelog generation; golden-file snapshot tests for results.json and results.csv. - Docs: README section on "Public artifacts" including intended consumers (docs site, marketing site, results service) and guidance for bumping schema_version.

Non-goals / guardrails: - No changes to how metrics are computed (latency/$ normalization stays in PR-4821). This is only an export + contract layer. - No PII / sensitive IDs are included; this PR intentionally only exports aggregated results and high-level config descriptors. (Redaction enforcement is handled separately; see follow-up PR-4873.)

Consumer contract (high level): - results.json includes: schema_version, dataset_id, generated_at, dimensions (model/region/workload/context_bucket), metrics (p50/p95 latency_ms, throughput_toks_per_s, cost_per_1m_tokens_usd, latency_per_dollar), and method flags (streaming=false for this dataset, caching_policy_id, batching_policy_id). - results.csv is a 1-row-per-dimension record intended for quick charting; it shares metric names with results.json where possible.

Rollout plan: - Land exporter behind the CLI flag; CI will run exports on the small smoke dataset. - Web team (Felix) will consume artifacts from a pinned schema_version and fail fast if schema_version mismatches expected major version. - Once docs site wiring is merged, we will enable exports in the full region sweep pipeline.

Risks: - Schema churn during methodology tweaks. Mitigation: semver schema_version + changelog.json + downstream contract tests. - Size of JSON bundle if we add too many slices. Mitigation: exporter supports filtering dimensions and splitting outputs per region/model in a follow-up if needed.
commits: export: add public artifacts exporter (json + csv) with stable ordering
schema: introduce public_results.schema.json and schema_version plumbing
cli: add --export-public-artifacts and --public-artifacts-dir
tests: golden snapshots for results.json/results.csv + schema validation
docs: README notes on schema bump + changelog expectations
source.conversation:
Felix Schneider (review): Can we guarantee backwards compatibility for the web table? I need a clear contract for column names and a way to detect a breaking change without parsing the whole file.

Rina Kobayashi (author): Added schema_version at top-level for JSON and as a commented header line for CSV ("# schema_version=1.0.0"). Also added changelog.json + a contract test that asserts the CSV header matches the schema.

Mei Lin (review): Please ensure determinism—row ordering and float formatting. Otherwise we’ll get noisy diffs and flaky cache behavior.

Rina: Updated exporter to sort by (model_id, region_id, workload_id, context_bucket) and format floats with fixed precision (latency_ms: 3 decimals, cost_usd: 6 decimals, throughput: 4 decimals). Added golden snapshots and made tests tolerant only within those formatting rules.

Nadia Rahman (review): For latency_per_dollar, can we document the exact unit? People will interpret it differently (ms per $ vs seconds per $). Also make sure we don’t rename existing metric keys without a major bump.

Rina: Documented units in README and in schema field descriptions: latency_per_dollar_ms_per_usd. Kept key name "latency_per_dollar" for continuity, but added "latency_per_dollar_unit" in manifest.json for explicitness. Also added a schema bump policy section (major bump on rename/remove).

Logan Wright (review): Please add a manifest field for git_sha and runner_version. Web ingestion needs build provenance.

Rina: Added git_sha, runner_version, and prompt_set_id to manifest.json. Also included generated_at (UTC ISO-8601) across artifacts.

Elliot Price (review): Can we add a short "How to bump schema_version" checklist? This will be referenced by docs/PMM.

Rina: Added README checklist + changelog.json format spec (fields: from_version, to_version, breaking, changes[]).

Final approvals: Felix ✅, Mei ✅, Nadia ✅, Logan ✅, Elliot ✅
source.checks:
CI summary (required): - unit-tests (python) ✅ - schema-validation ✅ - lint/format (ruff) ✅ - golden-snapshots ✅ - build-artifacts (smoke export) ✅

Notable runs: First CI run failed golden snapshot due to non-deterministic dict ordering in JSON writer; fixed by canonical ordering + stable sort and re-ran successfully.
source.release_notes: Benchmark pipeline can now export versioned public artifacts (results.json/results.csv) with schema_version, manifest, and changelog to support the interactive benchmark web page ingestion.

source.title: Stability gates: auto-rerun on p95 variance threshold + flaky-run CI coverage
source.author: Rina Kobayashi
source.description: Context: We’re seeing run-to-run instability in p95 latency on some model/region pairs (queueing effects, transient noisy neighbors, kernel selection differences). This PR adds a stability gate to the benchmark pipeline that detects high variance in p95 latency and automatically re-runs the affected slice up to a bounded limit. Goal: reduce noise in published aggregates while preserving the raw evidence trail (we keep the original run results and annotate what happened). What’s included: (1) Variance detection on repeated trials for each (model, region, workload, concurrency) slice, based on p95 latency. (2) Auto rerun when variance exceeds a configurable threshold, with a cap on reruns and explicit status reporting. (3) Manifest + result artifacts include stability metadata (variance %, trials, rerun_count, stability_status, stability_notes). (4) CI coverage: simulated flaky runs with deterministic jitter to ensure the gate triggers and produces stable, explainable output. Default behavior: run N trials (default N=5) per slice; compute p95 per trial; compute variance metric = (max(p95)-min(p95))/median(p95). If variance_metric > threshold (default 0.08 = 8%), schedule rerun for that slice, up to max_reruns (default 2). If still unstable after max reruns, mark slice as UNSTABLE and keep it in output with stability_status=UNSTABLE (so downstream publish steps can choose to exclude or flag it explicitly). Config: new runner flags --stability-trials, --stability-threshold, --stability-max-reruns, --stability-disable (for local debugging). Notes: This does not change the latency/$ computation; it only governs which trials are considered stable and how reruns are executed/recorded. Follow-ups: integrate with region sweep runner to prioritize reruns in the same reservation window; wire stability_status into the publish checklist (Confluence validation checklist) so Marketing only pulls STABLE slices for public tables unless explicitly approved.
commits: bench: add StabilityGate evaluation + rerun planner
bench: persist per-trial p95 and stability annotations into manifest
cli: add stability flags and defaults (trials/threshold/max-reruns)
ci: add flaky-run simulation tests w/ deterministic jitter
docs: update benchmark runner README w/ stability gate semantics
files_changed: schemas/run_manifest.schema.json (stability fields: status, variance_metric, rerun_count, trial_count)
tests/test_stability_gate.py (unit tests for thresholding and caps)
docs/runner.md (new flags + how to interpret UNSTABLE slices)
source.conversation: Jonas Weber (review): I like the direction, but please make sure we don’t silently ‘fix’ bad numbers. We should (a) keep original trials, (b) cap reruns, and (c) clearly mark UNSTABLE when we hit the cap. Also: can we choose a variance metric that is easy to explain to non-ML folks? Rina (author): Agree. Updated to always persist per-trial p95s, added stability_status=UNSTABLE when max_reruns exceeded, and changed metric to (max-min)/median which is easier to describe than CV. Claire Dubois (review): Two asks: (1) add a --stability-disable flag for debugging, (2) ensure rerun slices keep the same prompt set seed (we can’t let rerun change workload distribution). Rina: Added --stability-disable and pinned the prompt_set_seed into the slice key; reruns reuse the same seed and prompt indices. Nadia Rahman (review): Make sure the rerun logic doesn’t alter the cost attribution window. Some of our $/token normalization assumes fixed sampling. Also please add a note to the manifest so downstream knows which trials are ‘counted’ in the aggregate. Rina: Rerun doesn’t touch cost accounting; it only changes which trial set is used for the aggregated p95. Added manifest fields counted_trial_ids and stability_notes explaining selection. Sean Gallagher (review): CI coverage is good; please ensure flaky simulation test is deterministic and won’t cause CI to intermittently fail. Also add a small timeout guard so rerun planning can’t explode runtime if something is consistently unstable. Rina: Flaky sim uses fixed RNG seed; added runtime guard: max_total_attempts per job and early abort with UNSTABLE status if exceeded. Jonas (final): Looks good. Please add one more doc line recommending threshold ranges for different workloads. Rina: Added guidance (5–10% typical, 12% for high concurrency stress). Approved.

Goal: reduce noise in published aggregates while preserving the raw evidence trail (we keep the original run results and annotate what happened). What’s included: (1) Variance detection on repeated trials for each (model, region, workload, concurrency) slice, based on p95 latency. (2) Auto rerun when variance exceeds a configurable threshold, with a cap on reruns and explicit status reporting. (3) Manifest + result artifacts include stability metadata (variance %, trials, rerun_count, stability_status, stability_notes). (4) CI coverage: simulated flaky runs with deterministic jitter to ensure the gate triggers and produces stable, explainable output. Default behavior: run N trials (default N=5) per slice; compute p95 per trial; compute variance metric = (max(p95)-min(p95))/median(p95). If variance_metric > threshold (default 0.08 = 8%), schedule rerun for that slice, up to max_reruns (default 2). If still unstable after max reruns, mark slice as UNSTABLE and keep it in output with stability_status=UNSTABLE (so downstream publish steps can choose to exclude or flag it explicitly). Config: new runner flags --stability-trials, --stability-threshold, --stability-max-reruns, --stability-disable (for local debugging). Notes: This does not change the latency/$ computation; it only governs which trials are considered stable and how reruns are executed/recorded. Follow-ups: integrate with region sweep runner to prioritize reruns in the same reservation window; wire stability_status into the publish checklist (Confluence validation checklist) so Marketing only pulls STABLE slices for public tables unless explicitly approved.

source.title: Publish baseline vs quant-profile cost/token + latency deltas (CSV summaries + artifacts)
source.author: Jonas Weber
source.description:
Context: As part of the Quantization Profile Library rollout, we need a repeatable, reviewable set of benchmark outputs that quantify the impact of enabling a published quantization profile vs baseline precision on (a) unit economics (approx cost/token) and (b) latency/throughput across supported GPU SKUs. This PR adds a small publishing pipeline in benchmark-lab that generates normalized CSV summaries per model/profile/GPU and uploads both raw run logs and the rolled-up tables as CI artifacts. The goal is to let Applied ML + Runtime + Platform point to a single source of truth for “expected deltas” used by Console previews, rollout gates, and incident triage.

What’s included: (1) A new runner entrypoint that executes baseline + profile-enabled scenarios back-to-back with consistent warmup and pinned runtime build/commit; (2) CSV summaries under artifacts/quant_profiles/ summarizing p50/p95 end-to-end latency, decode latency, tokens/sec, VRAM peak, and derived cost/token; (3) A stable “schema_version” for the CSV format plus embedded metadata columns (model_id, variant, profile_id, profile_version, gpu_sku, runtime_commit, run_id, timestamp); (4) A README documenting how to reproduce locally and how to interpret cost/token.

Cost/token methodology: We compute cost/token as (gpu_hourly_usd / tokens_per_hour). gpu_hourly_usd comes from a static price table in this repo (documented as “estimates for comparison only”, not customer billing). tokens_per_hour is taken from measured tokens/sec after warmup and excluding initial prefill window. This is meant for relative deltas between baseline and profile-enabled on the same hardware + workload; absolute numbers will vary by fleet utilization and batching.

Workloads: This PR publishes deltas for the standard quant-profile perf suite workloads (short chat, long-context chat, and tool-call shaped prompts) at fixed concurrency points (1, 4, 16) for A100-80GB, H100-80GB, and L40S-48GB. Models currently included are the first beta targets (internal IDs): llama3.1-8b-instruct (x2), llama3.1-70b-instruct (x1), and qwen2.5-32b-instruct (x1) where profiles exist.

Example excerpt (see full CSV artifacts):
- llama3.1-8b-instruct:x2 INT8 profile (A100-80GB, conc=16, long-context): tokens/sec +22.8%, p95 e2e latency -6.1%, derived cost/token -18.6%
- llama3.1-70b-instruct:x1 FP8 mixed profile (H100-80GB, conc=4, short chat): tokens/sec +15.2%, p95 e2e latency -3.4%, derived cost/token -12.9%
- qwen2.5-32b-instruct:x1 INT8 profile (L40S-48GB, conc=16, tool-call): tokens/sec +9.7%, p95 e2e latency +2.1% (tail sensitivity), derived cost/token -7.8% (flagged for follow-up; see notes in README)

Notes / guardrails: This PR does not change any production defaults. Benchmarks are informational and intended to help validate expected savings and catch perf regressions. Profile compatibility is assumed pre-validated; this runner will fail fast if the runtime reports fallback or incompatibility (we export fallback_rate and enforce it == 0 for benchmark runs).

Follow-ups: (a) Add a lightweight “trend” job that compares latest results vs previous main commit and comments on PRs that touch runtime quantization paths; (b) Expand model coverage as new profiles are published; (c) Optionally export a JSON snapshot for Console preview ingestion once the Console team finalizes the ingestion path.

Screenshots/artifacts: CI run for this PR uploads: raw_run_logs.tgz, quant_profile_summary.csv, quant_profile_summary_by_gpu.csv, and per-model CSVs under artifacts/quant_profiles/<model_id>/.

Risk: Low. Changes are isolated to benchmark-lab scripts and CI. No customer-facing behavior change.

Refs: Confluence perf suite doc (quantization-profile-perf-benchmark-suite), runtime metrics naming from redwood/pr-18512, and rollout requirements from prd-quantization-profile-library-opt-in.
commits: benchmark: add quant profile benchmark runner + scenario matrix
benchmarks: add gpu price table + derived cost/token computation
artifacts: publish normalized CSV summaries (schema v1)
ci: upload artifacts/quant_profiles outputs on workflow completion
docs: add README for reproducing quant-profile delta runs
conversation: Hiro Tanaka (review): The tokens/sec jump looks plausible but I want to make sure we’re excluding the warmup and prefill window consistently. Can you clarify what window is used for tokens/sec and decode latency? Also suggest adding runtime_commit to the CSV to make bisects easier.

Jonas Weber (author): Good call. Currently we drop the first 60s warmup and compute tokens/sec over the remaining steady-state window; decode latency is taken from runtime metric decode_latency_ms aggregated over the same window. I pushed a commit adding runtime_commit + run_id + timestamp columns and documented the steady-state definition in README.

Nadia Rahman (review): For cost/token, please add a disclaimer that the $/hr table is for relative comparisons only. Also can we add schema_version as an explicit column so downstream tooling can detect changes?
Jonas Weber (author): Added both: schema_version column (v1) and a prominent disclaimer in README + schema_v1.md that cost/token is derived from an internal comparison table and not billing.

Arjun Desai (review): Can we export a per-profile summary table that shows delta vs baseline (percent) rather than only absolute numbers? That would make it easier to share with Console/PM without people doing spreadsheet math.

Jonas Weber (author): Added quant_profile_summary_by_gpu.csv which includes baseline vs profile rows plus computed delta columns (pct) for key metrics. Kept the raw absolute tables as well for auditing.
Logan Wright (review): Any concerns about metric naming drift? We’ve had a few runtime renames recently. If the runner can’t find the metric, it should fail loudly rather than publish partial data.
Jonas Weber (author): Implemented validate_metrics_present() and a hard fail if any required metric is missing; also added validate_no_fallback.py to enforce fallback_rate == 0.
Connor O'Brien (review): CI runtime for the workflow is ~42 minutes on the shared runner. Is that acceptable? Can we reduce matrix size for PR checks and keep the full matrix on main/nightly?

Jonas Weber (author): Updated workflow: PR runs a reduced matrix (single model + A100 + conc=4/16); main branch runs full matrix and uploads artifacts. PR artifacts still include CSV schema + sample outputs.

source.title: introduce-mini-deterministic-benchmark-corpus-and-sparse-cpu-probe-reporter
source.author: Riley Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a small deterministic benchmark corpus and a low-overhead sparse CPU probe reporter for microprofile comparisons. No user-facing API changes.
source.description:
Motivation: We lack a compact, deterministic benchmark fixture that reproduces common short-sequence hotpaths (latency-sensitive generations of length 8-64 tokens) while being cheap to run in CI and on local dev machines. Current suites are heavyweight and noisy; this PR introduces a curated mini corpus plus an experimental sparse CPU probe reporter that samples kernel-level wall-clock for a small set of annotated hotspots. The goal is faster benches that still exercise scheduling, KV-cache priming, and transient batch assembly so we can catch regressions early.

What this PR contains (high-level):
- mini_bench_corpus/: a compact set (12 sessions) of synthetic and real prompt traces sampled from production anonymized logs, normalized for determinism (fixed tokenization seeds, pinned random seeds, and controlled primer lengths).
- runner/mini_runner.py: lightweight harness that spins up the same runtime used by larger benches but with strict timing windows and a deterministic sampler to remove non-deterministic jitter.
- reporters/sparse_cpu_probe.py: an opt-in reporter that attaches a short-lived wall-clock probe to specific runtime phases (tokenize, model_dispatch, kv_priming, generate_step) and emits per-phase histograms only when the probe triggers (1% sampling by default). Designed to avoid adding noise to general benches.
- ci/mini-bench-workflow.yaml: a small GitHub Actions workflow that runs the corpus on PRs with a ~6 minute budget; it compares p50/p95 against a baseline artifact and fails on regressions above threshold.

Design and rationale:
Determinism: each trace is annotated with a seed and a deterministic tokenization path; the runner enforces identical preconditions (no background threads, CPU governor hint) so runs are repeatable within CI environment variance bounds.
Sparse probing: instead of instrumenting every run, the reporter samples a tiny fraction and attaches a reproducible trace ID so if we see a regressed sample we can re-run the same trace at higher fidelity. This keeps overall noise low and avoids skewing aggregate numbers.
Compatibility: The code uses existing bench harness interfaces (Runner and Reporter base classes) so it's a non-invasive addition and can be toggled from larger suites.

Commits (summary):
- Add mini corpus with 12 session JSONs and deterministic token metadata.
- Implement mini_runner harness with deterministic scheduler and CLI flags (--seed, --reps, --noisy=false).
- Add sparse_cpu_probe reporter that uses perf_event_open fallback to a tight wall-clock sampler when perf unavailable.
- CI workflow to run the mini suite on PRs and store artifact diffs.
- Tests for determinism (unit test asserts repeatable token sequences across runs) and integration test for reporter output schema.

Files changed (top-level):
- benchmark-lab/mini_bench_corpus/ (12 files added)
- benchmark-lab/runner/mini_runner.py (+450 lines)
- benchmark-lab/reporters/sparse_cpu_probe.py (+180 lines)
- benchmark-lab/tests/test_mini_determinism.py (+120 lines)
- benchmark-lab/ci/mini-bench-workflow.yaml (+60 lines)
- docs/bench.md (small addition linking the mini suite)
- README updates to document how to reproduce sampled failures locally

Testing and CI:
- Local unit tests added (pytest). Determinism test asserts identical token hashes across 3 runs with different wall-time but identical seeds.
- Integration regression check runs three repeated executions and computes p50/p95; CI workflow uploads an artifact 'mini-bench-baseline' on the first green run and compares subsequent runs to it.
- GitHub Actions matrix contains: ubuntu-latest (cpu-only), and an optional docker GPU job (manual).
- CI results: first run succeeded, a flaky timer test failed on second run due to CI jitter and was fixed by tighter sleep calibration; final CI status: pass.

Review thread (highlights):
- Mariana Lopez: "Nice small suite — can we reduce the default sampling from 1% to 0.5% to further lower overhead in cross-repo CI?"
- Riley Chen (author): "I tried 0.5% in an earlier iteration and saw loss of signal for 1-2 regressions; leaving at 1% for now but made sampling a CLI/config flag and added documentation on how to lower it per workflow."
- Ethan Park: "Can the reporter fall back gracefully when perf_event_open is not permitted in constrained runners? Also please ensure the reporter doesn't change scheduling affinity."
- Riley Chen: "Added fallback to monotonic wall-clock read with tighter sampling window; reporter only reads timestamps and never sets affinity. Added an integration test to validate behavior on restricted containers."
- Nora Singh: "Tests look good, but add a badge and baseline artifact upload so we can track long-term drift."
- Riley Chen: "Added artifact upload and a short section in docs/bench.md recommending baseline refresh cadence (monthly or after major compiler/toolchain upgrades)."

Performance & safety notes:
- This suite is explicitly not intended for capacity planning; it's a fast signal suite to detect regressions in hotpaths. For throughput/occupancy capacity planning, continue using the larger multi-stage suites.
- Reporter uses sampling with minimal overhead (<0.5% observed in local microbench runs).

How to reproduce a sampled failure locally:
1) Use runner/mini_runner.py --seed <trace-seed> --reps 10 --reporter sparse_cpu_probe --trace-id <id>
2) If the reporter indicated a regression sample, pass --high-fidelity to re-run that trace with full instrumentation and KV-cache warmup.

Follow-ups/TODOs:
- Add automated upload of sample traces to the artifact store for easier bisecting. (ENG-2178)
- Wire a dashboard in Redwood Console that surfaces mini-bench p50/p95 trends over time. (ENG-2104)
- Experiment with adaptive sampling rates tied to observed variance.

Merge notes:
- Squash-merged to main after final approval from Mariana and Ethan. Release notes added to the repo changelog. No configuration defaults were changed for existing suites.

- ci/mini-bench-workflow.yaml: a small GitHub Actions workflow that runs the corpus on PRs with a ~6 minute budget; it compares p50/p95 against a baseline artifact and fails on regressions above threshold.

Design and rationale:
Determinism: each trace is annotated with a seed and a deterministic tokenization path; the runner enforces identical preconditions (no background threads, CPU governor hint) so runs are repeatable within CI environment variance bounds.
Sparse probing: instead of instrumenting every run, the reporter samples a tiny fraction and attaches a reproducible trace ID so if we see a regressed sample we can re-run the same trace at higher fidelity. This keeps overall noise low and avoids skewing aggregate numbers.
Compatibility: The code uses existing bench harness interfaces (Runner and Reporter base classes) so it's a non-invasive addition and can be toggled from larger suites.

Commits (summary):
- Add mini corpus with 12 session JSONs and deterministic token metadata.
- Implement mini_runner harness with deterministic scheduler and CLI flags (--seed, --reps, --noisy=false).
- Add sparse_cpu_probe reporter that uses perf_event_open fallback to a tight wall-clock sampler when perf unavailable.
- CI workflow to run the mini suite on PRs and store artifact diffs.
- Tests for determinism (unit test asserts repeatable token sequences across runs) and integration test for reporter output schema.

Files changed (top-level):
- benchmark-lab/mini_bench_corpus/ (12 files added)
- benchmark-lab/runner/mini_runner.py (+450 lines)
- benchmark-lab/reporters/sparse_cpu_probe.py (+180 lines)
- benchmark-lab/tests/test_mini_determinism.py (+120 lines)
- benchmark-lab/ci/mini-bench-workflow.yaml (+60 lines)
- docs/bench.md (small addition linking the mini suite)
- README updates to document how to reproduce sampled failures locally

Testing and CI:
- Local unit tests added (pytest). Determinism test asserts identical token hashes across 3 runs with different wall-time but identical seeds.
- Integration regression check runs three repeated executions and computes p50/p95; CI workflow uploads an artifact 'mini-bench-baseline' on the first green run and compares subsequent runs to it.
- GitHub Actions matrix contains: ubuntu-latest (cpu-only), and an optional docker GPU job (manual).
- CI results: first run succeeded, a flaky timer test failed on second run due to CI jitter and was fixed by tighter sleep calibration; final CI status: pass.

Review thread (highlights):
- Mariana Lopez: "Nice small suite — can we reduce the default sampling from 1% to 0.5% to further lower overhead in cross-repo CI?"
- Riley Chen (author): "I tried 0.5% in an earlier iteration and saw loss of signal for 1-2 regressions; leaving at 1% for now but made sampling a CLI/config flag and added documentation on how to lower it per workflow."
- Ethan Park: "Can the reporter fall back gracefully when perf_event_open is not permitted in constrained runners? Also please ensure the reporter doesn't change scheduling affinity."
- Riley Chen: "Added fallback to monotonic wall-clock read with tighter sampling window; reporter only reads timestamps and never sets affinity. Added an integration test to validate behavior on restricted containers."

1) Use runner/mini_runner.py --seed <trace-seed> --reps 10 --reporter sparse_cpu_probe --trace-id <id>

source.title: Introduce timebox seeder and hierarchical telemetry compactifier for long-run benchmarks
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a timeboxed seeding harness and a multiresolution telemetry rollup writer. Improves long-run telemetry footprint and produces more realistic warm-cache steady-state profiles.
source.description:
Motivation: Long-duration benchmark runs in Benchmark Lab are noisy and expensive to store because we capture full-event traces and per-sample payloads for every iteration. Two common pain points: (1) realistic warm-cache steady-state is expensive to reach deterministically across different sequence-length mixes; (2) raw telemetry volume for long runs (multi-hour stress / soak) is large, which slows downstream analysis and inflates storage costs.

Design & changes: Introduce a timebox seeder (timebox-seeder) that drives a configurable, bounded priming stage prior to each measurement epoch. The seeder supports: target distributions (seq-length, token-priming prefixes, model-mix), controlled eviction pressure knobs, and deterministic seeding modes (seed schedules can be reused across runs for reproducibility). Parallel to the seeder, add a hierarchical telemetry compactifier (rollup-telemetry) which consumes sample-level events and emits multiresolution rollups: fixed-window histograms for latency/queueing, sparse top-k hotpath counts (kernel and code-path labels), and a compact KV-cache delta summary. Rollups are time-aligned to the benchmark epochs and include provenance tags so downstream reporters can reconstruct coarse timelines for debugging.

API & operator UX: New CLI targets: `benchctl seed --profile <yaml>` and `benchctl rollup --out <path>`. We also added two YAML profiles under bench/configs/profiles/ to demonstrate common web-chat and search-like mixes. The default CI benchmark workflow now runs a 2-minute timebox seed before each steady-state sampling window. The new rollup files are gzipped ndjson, with one record per rollup-epoch.

Results: On the representative multi-model chat workload (mix: 0.7 single-turn short, 0.3 multi-turn long) we observed: - telemetry footprint reduction: ~40x (raw events -> rollups) for a 2h soak; - steady-state p95 latency stability improved across repeated runs (stdev reduced by ~18%) because the timebox seeder produces consistent cache state; - wall-clock time to reach measurable steady-state reduced by 30% in our lab when using a 2-minute timebox profile vs no explicit seeding.

Backward compatibility: Rollup outputs are additive; we kept the raw capture path behind a flag so existing analysis pipelines continue to work. The seeder can be disabled per-run. No changes to existing benchmark schema were required.

Operational notes: Rollup writer includes a size threshold which auto-rotates files when the gzipped rollup file exceeds the configured limit (default 64MB). We added a lightweight sanity-checker binary that validates rollup provenance and TTL.

Why this is different from previous work: This PR focuses on two pragmatic operational bottlenecks: deterministic warm-cache priming in a timeboxed, reproducible way (as opposed to ad-hoc long warmups), and reducing telemetry storage/ingest costs for long soak/soak-like workloads while preserving actionable signals for regressions and hotspots.

Link to related Linear items: ENG-2384 (timebox seeding design), ENG-2401 (rollup format and storage savings).
source.commits_summary: 3 commits: (1) initial timebox-seeder implementation with profile loader and CLI; (2) rollup-telemetry writer + ndjson gzip serializer; (3) CI workflow updates, profiles, and tests. Each commit includes unit tests and small integration harnesses that run in the bench CI pool.
discussion: Diego Morales (reviewer): Can we add a deterministic RNG seed option so results are bit-reproducible across runs?
Author (Ava Chen): Added `--deterministic-seed` flag that accepts an int; documented in profiles. This was implemented in commit 2.
Priya Nair (reviewer): Rollup format looks good; add a schema version field so we can evolve without breaking consumers.
Author (Ava Chen): Added `schema_version` to each rollup record (default "v1"). Also added a migration note in docs/bench/rollup.md.
Jonah Brooks (approver): Verified end-to-end in mini-cluster. Observed ~35-45x telemetry reduction for 1h soak. Approving pending CI flakiness fixes.
CI (bench-integration): integration-tests failed on first run due to intermittent network flake in bench CI; rerun succeeded.
Diego Morales: requested adding a small example in bench/recipes showing how to rehydrate rollups into timeline views.
Author (Ava Chen): Added recipe and a tiny demo script that reconstructs epoch timelines from rollups.

lint: pass with minor format change auto-applied

source.title: holo-surge-sequencer-and-crossframe-occupancy-exporter
source.author: Marina Lopez
source.release_notes: Adds a new sequencer (holo-surge) for generating temporally correlated microbursts and a crossframe occupancy exporter for GPU/kernel-level attribution. No user-facing API changes.
source.description:
Motivation: we observed sustained mid-tail p95 instability when replaying multi-sequence conversational traffic with interleaved priming and varied KV cache hits. Existing synthetic runners produce either Poisson-like bursts or deterministic ramps; they do not emulate the fractal-like, multi-scale surges we see in production that cause cross-frame resource contention and transient kernel occupancy spikes. This PR introduces the 'holo-surge' sequencer (a compact workload generator that composes deterministic priming, stochastic microbursts, and long-period envelope modulation) and a crossframe occupancy exporter that records per-step GPU occupancy signatures and correlates them with logical request frames for downstream reporters.

What this change does: adds a new sequencer (cmd/holo-surge) and a reporting pipeline that serializes occupancy traces in a compact delta format compatible with the benchmark-lab downstream stats pipeline. The exporter uses platform-specific probes (NVIDIA SMI sampling hook or synthetic occupancy estimator when SMI is unavailable) and annotates traces with primer-id, sequence-hash, and KV cache-hit ratio. The sequencer supports deterministic seeds, phase locking across parallel nodes, and variable inter-sequence offsets for interference testing.

Checklist: [x] design doc (ENG-3124) reviewed; [x] unit tests for sequencer RNG and phase-lock logic; [x] integration test that runs holo-surge against mini-cluster and validates exporter schema; [x] perf benchmark comparing p50/p95/p99 and occupancy histograms before/after (summary below); [ ] follow-up: dashboard panels and alerting queries (separate PR).

Testing and validation: ran 5 controlled cohorts on the perf-lab cluster (4x A100 nodes) with identical model and prompt mixes to reproduce production-like priming distribution. All CI checks and integration heartbeat succeeded. Exported traces were consumed by an offline analyzer and matched expected event counts and envelope shapes.

Performance summary (representative): baseline sequencer (seq-ramp) vs holo-surge (same aggregated qps): p50 delta: +3ms, p95 delta: +18ms, p99 delta: +42ms; peak transient GPU occupancy increased by ~12% during synchronized microbursts, with tail correlation to KV cache misses. These numbers are included as CSV artifacts in the PR build.

Usage: examples/holo-surge/README.md demonstrates how to run a single-node holo-surge with deterministic seed, how to enable SMI sampling, and how to post-process the delta-occupancy traces with existing reporters. The sequencer exposes knobs: --envelope-period, --microburst-shape, --phase-lock, --primer-set, --seed, and an output sink compatible with our tracer service.

Related: builds on ideas from ENG-2710 (multi-sequence priming) and pairs with the compact-flame exporter work (PR #50271) for visualization.

Notes: the exporter includes a lightweight fallback estimator when SMI is not available; this avoids hard dependency on privileged runtime, but estimator accuracy is lower (documented in the README). This work intentionally does not change any public HTTP endpoints or the benchmark-lab stable dataset formats; it adds a new export flavor 'occupancy-delta-v1'.

Marina Lopez: yes, added a runtime probe switch and estimator fallback in commit 2 (reply)

Marina Lopez: added a section in examples/holo-surge/README.md explaining estimator bounds and a link to the diag CSVs (reply)

source.title: Steady-state slew-rate prober and latency-heatmap serializer
source.author: Aisha Patel
commits: initial: add slew-rate prober harness and config schema
perf: implement steady-state detector and adaptive sampling window
report: heatmap serializer and compact binary layout for telemetry export
tests: add synthetic microburst scenarios and unit tests for detector
docs: usage + tuning guide for slew-rate thresholds
fix: tighten thread-safety around sample buffer access
chore: bump benchmark-lab version to 0.18.4
source.description: Context: Production traffic exhibits sudden arrival-rate changes that current benchmarks treat as either isolated spikes or steady-state; we lacked a lightweight prober to characterize slew (rate-of-change) and to mark steady-state windows for downstream aggregations. This PR introduces a 'slew-rate prober' that emits time-series markers and a compact latency-heatmap serializer to efficiently export per-window histograms for postprocessing. Motivation: Provide an automated way to (1) detect sustained ramp-up/down phases, (2) isolate true steady-state windows for p95/p99 comparison across builds, and (3) export telemetry in a bandwidth-efficient format so long-run batch analyses are cheaper. High-level changes: added a new python prober harness (slew_rate_prober.py) with a configurable EWMA-based slew estimator and a hysteresis-driven steady-state detector; implemented adaptive sampling that reduces per-sample overhead during stable windows; added a Go-based heatmap_serializer that packs per-bucket counts into a compact TLV-like binary for faster ingestion by the analytics pipeline; added unit/integration tests and a tuning doc. Checklist: - [x] implement detector and sampling - [x] unit tests for detector edge cases - [x] create synthetic workloads (microburst/sustained-ramp) - [x] serializer with compatible schema - [x] docs and example config - [x] basic CI coverage and flake checks Backwards compatibility: The prober is opt-in and the serializer supports both new compact binary and legacy JSON exports via a feature flag in configs. Performance: local microbenchmarks show the prober adds <1% CPU overhead at 10k RPS on a c5.4xlarge equivalent and reduces exported telemetry size by ~6x versus naive JSON histograms. Risks & mitigations: false-positive steady-state detection on oscillatory traffic; mitigated by tunable hysteresis and a minimum-window-duration guard. Related work: follows ideas from ENG-3245 (steady-state isolation) and complements ENG-3310 (heatmap ingestion pipeline).
review_comments: Lena Zhao: Nice concise API; please add a short blurb in benchmark-lab/README about opt-in probes; also can we expose sampling rate as a runtime flag for dedicated runs?
Aisha Patel (author): Added README blurb and exposed sampling_rate in slew_defaults.yaml; documented runtime override in docs.
Marcus Li: The detector logic looks good but I'm worried about allocation churn in the serializer; can we reuse buffers and avoid per-bucket allocations?
Aisha Patel (author): Reworked serializer to use a preallocated byte buffer and streaming encode; updated benchmarks to show allocation drop from ~12MB to ~1.2MB per export.
Priya Natarajan: CI flake on test_slew_detector at p99 timing margin; suggest increasing tolerance or using deterministic clock in test harness.
Aisha Patel (author): Switched tests to deterministic fake clock and tightened assertions; reran CI.
Marcus Li: Approved after buffer reuse changes and tests stabilized.
Lena Zhao: Approved; documented recommended default thresholds for PL-eval runs.
source.release_notes: Add opt-in slew-rate prober to detect ramp phases and export compact latency heatmaps; reduces telemetry size and improves steady-state isolation for performance comparisons.

High-level changes: added a new python prober harness (slew_rate_prober.py) with a configurable EWMA-based slew estimator and a hysteresis-driven steady-state detector; implemented adaptive sampling that reduces per-sample overhead during stable windows; added a Go-based heatmap_serializer that packs per-bucket counts into a compact TLV-like binary for faster ingestion by the analytics pipeline; added unit/integration tests and a tuning doc. Checklist: - [x] implement detector and sampling - [x] unit tests for detector edge cases - [x] create synthetic workloads (microburst/sustained-ramp) - [x] serializer with compatible schema - [x] docs and example config - [x] basic CI coverage and flake checks Backwards compatibility: The prober is opt-in and the serializer supports both new compact binary and legacy JSON exports via a feature flag in configs. Performance: local microbenchmarks show the prober adds <1% CPU overhead at 10k RPS on a c5.4xlarge equivalent and reduces exported telemetry size by ~6x versus naive JSON histograms. Risks & mitigations: false-positive steady-state detection on oscillatory traffic; mitigated by tunable hysteresis and a minimum-window-duration guard. Related work: follows ideas from ENG-3245 (steady-state isolation) and complements ENG-3310 (heatmap ingestion pipeline).

source.title: Phase-locked traffic replayer and quantile-hashmap serializer
source.author: Amrita Singh
source.description:
Motivation: We frequently observe ephemeral microbursts that escape our existing replayers because sequence timing jitter masks phase relationships across concurrent sessions. This PR introduces a phase-locked loop (PLL) inspired traffic replayer that aligns request phases across synthetic sessions to deterministically reproduce multi-tenant interference patterns and kernel saturation events. It also adds a compact quantile-hashmap serializer for per-epoch quantile sketches so reporting pipelines can stream delta-updates without holding large in-memory sketches.

What this PR does (single-string summary): Adds a new lightweight replayer (bench/replayers/phase_locked_replayer.py) that can lock multiple synthetic sessions to a scheduler phase, implements deterministic seeding and jitter maps, exposes an API to pin phase offsets and slot widths, and integrates with the existing bench-run harness. Adds a quantile-hashmap serializer (bench/reporting/quantile_hashmap.py) which converts DDSketch-style buckets into a small hashmap keyed by quantile-range + epoch with fast merge/serialize semantics. Adds end-to-end perf tests and reporting pipelines to the `bench-run` CI job and a smoke dashboard exporter to persist compacted quantiles to the S3-compatible artifact store.

Why this is different: Existing replayers randomize inter-request spacing or replay wall-clock traces; the PLL replayer models phase coherence explicitly so we can reproduce pathological alignments (e.g., slot-synchronized bursts) which historically caused 2-3x p99 regressions on certain kernels. The quantile-hashmap serializer makes per-epoch quantile diffs cheap to emit, enabling finer-grained alerting without heavy storage or downstream aggregation work.

Checklist: 
- [x] New replayer implementation with deterministic tests
- [x] Quantile serializer with merge/serialize unit tests
- [x] Integration: bench-run job updated to exercise phase-locked scenarios
- [x] Examples: small scenario config and a README describing modes and knob semantics
- [x] Documentation: short design note added to docs/bench-lab/phase-locked.md

Interfaces and knobs: 
- replayer: bench.replayers.phase_locked.PhaseLockedReplayer(phase_period_ms, slot_width_ms, seed, jitter_map)
- run mode: bench-run --scenario phase-locked --concurrency 64 --pin-phase-offset 17
- serializer: QuantileHashmap(epoch_id, sketch_buckets) -> bytes (compact streamable diff)

High-level performance validation: 
- Synthetic reproduction of a previously flaky kernel-hotpath showed identical flamepoints across runs with locked phases (reproducibility > 99% for p99 hotspots).
- Storage: quantile-hashmap serializations reduced per-epoch telemetry size by ~6x vs full DDSketch exports for our default bucket set.

Included small illustrative code excerpt (for reviewers only):
- Phase locking loop core (pseudo):
  for phase_tick in range(0, duration_ms, phase_period_ms):
    for slot in slots:
      scheduled_time = phase_tick + (slot.index * slot_width_ms) + jitter_map[slot.id]
      schedule_request(session_for_slot(slot), scheduled_time)

Commit summary and intent is in the commits field below. All new code is feature-flagged behind `ENABLE_PHASE_REPLAYER` and defaults to off in prod runs.

Files of interest changed are listed below; diffs are small and focused. CI and perf runs were added to protect regressions.

source.title: sequence churn stressor and multiwindow latency mapper
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Context: We need a compact runner and reporting pipeline that exercises 'sequence churn'—the interaction between variable context window sizes, primer reuse, and short-lived KV cache entries that manifest as multi-window latency regressions in production. Motivation: Recent production traces showed p95 regressions correlated with bursts of short-context requests that evict hot KV entries, causing downstream longer-context requests to suffer. This PR introduces a workload generator (seq-churn-stressor) and a reporting component (multiwindow-latency-mapper) that: 1) reproduces realistic churn patterns across tenants and primer-length distributions, 2) collects per-window latency heatmaps (window sizes: 32, 64, 128, 256 tokens), 3) emits cohorted quantiles and an artifact CSV for cross-run comparison. Checklist: - [x] Implement stochastic primer/seed generator with tenant skew and TTL-driven KV eviction model - [x] Integrate with existing harness (benchmark-lab runner shim) - [x] Add multi-window aggregation and cohorting logic - [x] Add dashboards exporter (compact CSV + compressed JSON artifact) - [x] Unit tests for sampler and mapper - [x] Run on perf-lab cluster and validate telemetry Design summary: - seq_churn_stressor: a lightweight Python runner that uses session templates to produce sequences with a mixture of short (<=64) and long (>128) contexts. The sampler supports configurable churn_rate, primer_reuse_probability, tenant_skew, and TTL distribution to emulate KV cache churn. The runner emits events annotated with request_id, tenant_id, primer_len, window_size, and observed_token_latencies. - multiwindow_latency_mapper: a Go pipeline component that ingests runner events (via Kafka test topic in perf-lab), consolidates latencies into fixed windows (32/64/128/256), computes p50/p90/p95/p99 per-cohort (cohorts: primer_len_bucket, tenant_bucket, churn_bucket), and writes both a compact parquet-like JSON artifact and a one-line-per-sample CSV for easy diffing in CI. New telemetry: - metrics/seq_churn/request_count - metrics/seq_churn/kv_evictions - metrics/seq_churn/window_latency_p95 - histogram/window_token_latency{window_size} - artifact/storage path: s3://perf-artifacts/seq-churn/{run_id}/{artifact}.zip Backwards compatibility: Runner integrates with existing runner-shim; mapper exposes the same ingest schema as other reporters. No break to existing benchmarks. Commits: ["a1f7d2c Add seq_churn_stressor runner and sampler module", "b3e5c8f Implement multiwindow_mapper aggregation and CSV exporter", "c2d9a45 Add unit tests for churn sampler and cohort logic", "d0147b0 CI: add kafka test topic fixture and perf-lab job config", "e9f1f11 Docs: usage and dashboard example"] Files changed (high level): ["benchmark_lab/runners/seq_churn_stressor.py", "benchmark_lab/reporters/multiwindow_mapper/main.go", "benchmark_lab/reporters/multiwindow_mapper/mapper_test.go", "benchmark_lab/common/telemetry/schema.yaml", "perf-ci/jobs/seq-churn-perf.yaml", "docs/benchmark-lab/seq-churn-runbook.md", "tools/artifact-uploader/upload.py", "docs/dashboards/seq-churn-dashboard.json", "README-benchmarks.md"] Small illustrative diff snippets (only where helpful): - benchmark_lab/runners/seq_churn_stressor.py (sampling core): for session in sessions: primer = primer_pool.sample(primer_reuse_prob); window = choose_window(session); send_event(...) - multiwindow_mapper/main.go (aggregation key): key := fmt.Sprintf("%d|%s|%s", windowSize, cohort.PrimerBucket, cohort.TenantBucket) Testing / CI: - Added unit tests for primer reuse probability edge cases and mapper cohort bucketing. - CI initially failed on flaky kafka fixture (integration test timeout). Root cause: perf-ci cluster has tightened partitions; fixed by increasing consumer timeout and adding a small backoff. After re-run CI passed. - Ran three full perf-lab jobs (small/medium/large). Results: consistent increase in observable p95 for 128-window during high churn runs, validating the runner. Review conversation (high level): - Diego Alvarez (reviewer): "Can we make primer_reuse_probability configurable per-tenant? In some customers primer reuse is correlated with rate limits." - Maya Chen (author): "Added tenant_override in latest commit; defaults behave as before but can be set via --tenant-overrides file." - Priya Natarajan: "Mapper looks good; suggest adding a combined heatmap export for dashboarding." - Maya Chen: "Added heatmap JSON export and a sample Grafana panel in docs." - Evan Brooks: "CI flaky test fixed; lgtm once CI green." Merge outcome: merged via squash/merge. Release notes: "Introduce seq_churn_stressor runner and multiwindow latency mapper. Adds synthetic churn workload and artifacts to help reproduce and analyze multi-window tail regressions. Useful for debugging KV cache thrashing and primer-related p95 spikes." Rollout and next steps: - Add automated benchmark comparison job to PR gating (follow-up ENG-3025). - Hook parser into Optimize to suggest primer_reuse thresholds when churn detected. Links: - Runbook: ./docs/benchmark-lab/seq-churn-runbook.md - Linear: https://linear.app/company/issue/ENG-2981 - Perf artifacts sample: s3://perf-artifacts/seq-churn/sample-20241120.zip

b3e5c8f Implement multiwindow_mapper aggregation and CSV exporter

source.title: asymmetric-concurrency-skew-generator-and-delta-reporter
source.author: Priya Shah
source.description:
Motivation: production traffic often exhibits heavy user skew where a small fraction of sessions produce high token bursts and long-lived KV cache pressure. Existing synthetic runners in benchmark-lab model uniform sequence mixes or simple hot-spot priors; we need a compact harness that can: 1) generate temporally correlated bursty sessions with configurable skew parameters; 2) drive the serving runtime under asymmetric concurrency to exercise scheduling and KV cache eviction behaviors; and 3) produce a compact delta report that correlates latency/regression deltas with kernel-level sample points and concurrency slices so that regressions can be triaged to scheduling vs kernel choices.

This PR implements: a) an "asymmetric-concurrency-skew" workload generator (bench/chaos/asymmetric_skew.go + python harness) supporting: percentiles-of-sessions that become "bursters", configurable burst lengths, inter-burst backoff distributions, and an option to pin bursts to a subset of model shards to provoke scheduling hotspots; b) an interaction-replay adapter that converts production session traces into parametric generators for deterministic replay; c) a delta-reporter pipeline (reporting/delta_reporter.py) that ingests per-request telemetry and aggregates per-concurrency-slice p50/p95/p99, token-rate, and a new metric "tail_concurrency_skew"; d) a small profiling baseline target and flamepoint mapper that links sampled kernel stacks to delta report cohorts for quick root-cause inspection.

Checklist:
- [x] Workload generator implementation + unit tests
- [x] Replay adapter with deterministic seeding
- [x] Delta reporter with CSV and JSON outputs (schema documented)
- [x] CI integration: unit tests + small e2e smoke job in bench-lab CI matrix
- [x] Minimal docs: README with knobs and example Linear ticket templates

Commits summary: ["Initial asymmetric load generator and model-pin option", "add python delta reporter and CSV schema", "integration: add deterministic replay adapter and golden seed", "tests: add unit tests for skew generation", "docs: README and usage examples", "fix: edge-case in concurrency slice aggregation"]

Changed areas (high-level): ["bench/chaos/asymmetric_skew.go", "bench/harness/replay_adapter.py", "reporting/delta_reporter.py", "reporting/schema/v1.json", "bench/tests/test_asymmetric_skew.py", "bench/ci/bench_e2e_smoke.yml", "docs/bench/asymmetric_skew.md"]

Review conversation (high level):
- Ethan Park: "Can you add a deterministic seed path so CI replays are stable? Also, please explain how this interacts with KV cache checkpoints in the README."
- Priya Shah: "Added deterministic seeding and updated README with guidance for KV checkpointing and how to interpret tail_concurrency_skew. See latest commit."
- Marta Kline: "The delta report ties into flamepoints nicely, but we should limit the default retention of sampled stacks for bench runs to avoid storage blowup."
- Priya Shah: "Implemented a sampling cap and summarized the retention tradeoffs in the docs; default = 1000 stacks per run cohort."
- Diego Alvarez: "Approving once CI green and tests cover model-pin multicast path."

CI summary: all unit tests and bench smoke matrix passed on first run except one flaky test in bench/tests/test_kvcache_occupancy.py which was re-run and passed; full matrix green. Checks: ["golangci-lint: pass", "pytest: pass", "bench-smoke-e2e: pass", "docs-lint: pass"]

Performance notes and findings included in the PR: the asymmetric workload reproduces a scheduling-induced p99 spike when bursters exceed 8% of sessions for our current scheduler config; with the new scheduling hint (experimental) the p99 reduced by ~22% in a 4-GPU shard layout. The delta reporter makes it straightforward to attribute the regression to thread-starvation during KV cache eviction windows vs kernel selection for attention.

Merge notes: squash-merged after addressing reviewers' comments and adding seeding; release notes generated and attached.

Related work: ENG-4021 (scheduling experiments), bench-lab issue #413 (need for replayable skewed workloads), SUP-1291 (support ticket from customer experiencing tail spikes).

source.release_notes: Add asymmetric concurrency skew generator and delta reporter: new workload to reproduce and triage tail latency caused by user skew; delta reporter correlates latency deltas with kernel-sampled flamepoints and concurrency slices. Recommendation: enable sampling cap and use deterministic seed for CI replay.
source.notes_for_next_iteration: Add an option to export per-shard KV cache occupancy heatmaps and integrate with perf-canary flamepoint collector; consider a streaming mode for delta reports to feed console in near-real-time.
source.tagline: Introduce a deterministic, configurable skewed-workload generator plus a delta reporter to attribute tail regressions to scheduling vs kernel behavior for faster root-cause triage.
